title: Welcome
text:
I’m Sarah Kopitz, a student in the Master of Geomatics for Environmental Management (MGEM) program at the University of British Columbia. I’m interested in environmental work that connects land, forests, water, wildlife, and people.
I completed a BSc in Environmental Science (Specialization in Forest Ecology) at the University of Winnipeg. During my undergrad I became drawn to the spatial side of environmental work.
Geomatics stands out to me because it’s both technical and creative. I enjoy problem solving, building workflows, and finding clear ways to communicate results through maps and visuals.
Indigenous rights and land are important to me and they shape how I think about environmental work, including who benefits from environmental decisions and how knowledge is respected and represented.

title: Education
text:
Education
Master of Geomatics for Environmental Management (MGEM)
University of British Columbia
2025 – Present
Bachelor of Science (Environmental Science - Specialization in Forest Ecology)
University of Winnipeg
Completed 2025



Work Experience
Environmental Co-op Student – Forestry Branch
Manitoba Hydro
May 2023 – April 2025
Worked with the utility vegetation management team on a range of GIS, mapping, and data tasks. A large part of my role involved improving and automating workflows, including building an annual vegetation analysis in FME that integrates multiple datasets and produces priority ranking lists across Manitoba. I also created and maintained ArcGIS Online web maps and apps (Field Maps, Experience Builder, Dashboards), supported spatial QA/QC, and helped with field work when needed.
Technical Support Student – Property Department
Manitoba Hydro
May 2022 – April 2023
Provided GIS and data support within the Property Department. I reviewed and cleaned spatial and tabular datasets, ran analyses in ArcGIS Pro, and supported property-related work. One example was using ArcGIS Pro ModelBuilder to link hydro line spatial data with property information and environmental datasets for tracking and budget estimating automation.
Supervisor / Server / Bartender
Leopold’s Tavern
June 2020 – May 2024
Worked in a high volume, fast paced restaurant environment while regularly taking on supervisor shifts. In addition to serving and bartending, I coordinated staff during shifts, handled cash management and end of day reporting, and stepped in to resolve issues with guests or team members. The role required staying organized under pressure and balancing multiple responsibilities at once.



Research Experience
Undergraduate Student Research Award (USRA) – NSERC
University of Winnipeg
May 2025 – August 2025
Worked on a GIS and mapping project focused on phosphorus risk factors across Manitoba’s portion of the Lake Winnipeg watershed. I built and organized a set of spatial layers (sources, transport pathways, landscape factors), created maps to compare where these factors overlap, and developed an ArcGIS Online web map and StoryMap to communicate the results. I also assisted with field and lab work supporting other ongoing research projects in the lab.



Technical Skills
GIS & Geospatial:
ArcGIS Pro, ArcMap, ArcGIS Online (AGOL), QGIS
Coding & Automation:
FME, ArcGIS ModelBuilder, Arcade, R, basic Python and SQL
Remote Sensing:
Multispectral imagery, LiDAR data, time series analysis, raster processing
Other:
Data quality checks, workflow automation, map creation and design, Microsoft Office



Awards & Honors

Indigenous Graduate Fellowship (IGF), University of British Columbia (2025)

NSERC Undergraduate Student Research Award (USRA), University of Winnipeg (2025)

University of Winnipeg / Indspire Indigenous Award – Undergraduate (2023, 2024)
Academic Proficiency Scholarship, University of Winnipeg (2023)
Dean’s Honour List – Student of Highest Distinction, University of Winnipeg (2023, 2024)
Dean’s Honour List – Student of Distinction, University of Winnipeg (2023)




Professional Organizations

Canadian Institute of Forestry (April 2025 – Present)

title: Publications
text:
Peer-reviewed publications:
Despite listing them on your resume, it may also be pertinent to create a seperate tab for publications and reports. As your career progresses, this list may become quite long, so be sure to organize things. You may want to point to ‘most recent’ publications, or categorize things by project/topic.

title: Content & Deliverables
text:
Project Deliverable 1
This is a sample page where you can archive project deliverables.

title: MGEM
text:
Northern Manitoba
This project examines post-fire spectral recovery using Landsat time series. Normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) and normalized burn ratio (NBR) were used to describe how vegetation recovery changes over time after wildfire.
What I did

Built NDVI and NBR time series composites for the study area
Mapped burn severity using dNBR (pre- vs post-fire comparison)
Calculated recovery metrics from the time series (years to recovery and recovery rate/slope)
Compared recovery across burn severity classes and pre-fire land cover types
Produced maps and summary figures to support interpretation

VIEW R CODE AND PROJECT CONTENT




Duck Mountain, Manitoba
This was a two page proposal completed in a landscape ecology and management course. I outlined a landscape question and analysis plan and chose to use landscape metrics to describe how patterns could be measured.
The proposal examines how industrial logging patterns have changed over time in Forest Management License 3 (Duck Mountain, Manitoba) using Landsat based disturbance data (NTEMS).
What I proposed

Use NTEMS harvest disturbance data (1994–2022)
Reclass harvest-year layers into logged / not logged maps
Apply landscape metrics (PLAND, NP, AREA_MN/SD, CLUMPY)
Evaluate changes in patch number, patch size, and clustering
Interpret implications for landscape pattern and forest management

VIEW PROPOSAL




Alex Fraser Research Forest, British Columbia
This project focused on developing a suite of forest structural products from airborne LiDAR data. The goal was to move from raw point cloud data to forest metrics and mapped predictions across the study area.
What I did

Built a digital elevation model (DEM) and canopy height model (CHM) mosaic from LiDAR data
Modeled dominant height and above-ground biomass (AGB) using LiDAR metrics
Applied the models wall-to-wall to produce continuous prediction surfaces across the study area

VIEW R CODE




Malcolm Knapp Research Forest, British Columbia
This project focused on detecting and segmenting individual trees from LiDAR data using two different approaches.
What I did

Prepared and normalized LiDAR data for tree-level analysis
Segmented trees using Li et al. (2012) point-cloud method and Dalponte & Coomes (2016) CHM-based method

VIEW R CODE

  
  
  
    3D visualization of Dalponte (2016) individual tree segmentation at 0.5 m CHM resolution.
  





Nahmint Watershed, British Columbia
This project focused on defining Riparian Management Areas (RMAs) within a topographically complex watershed using a Digital Elevation Model (DEM) and hydrological tools.
What I did

Processed a DEM to remove sinks and model flow direction and accumulation

Extracted and classified stream networks using Strahler and Shreve ordering

Calculated stream gradient and width to determine fish-bearing potential

Assigned stream classes and applied reserve and management zone buffers using python field calculations

Delineated watersheds and generated contour lines

Produced a final map summarizing riparian management areas, stream networks, and terrain

VIEW FINAL MAP




Central British Columbia
This team based final project for a Python course focused on analyzing forest harvest cutblocks using spatial data processing and visualization techniques. The objective was to explore temporal and spatial harvest patterns and document the workflow in a reproducible notebook format.
What we did

Prepared and cleaned spatial datasets

Analyzed harvest patterns over time

Produced maps and summary visualizations

Organized and documented the final notebook workflow

VIEW NOTEBOOK

title: MGEM
text:
Undergraduate Project — USRA (Summer 2025)
Mapping Factors Influencing Phosphorus in Manitoba Streams (Lake Winnipeg Watershed)
Pre-MGEM!
Phosphorus is essential, but too much in freshwater can contribute to algae blooms and water quality issues. In this project, I mapped spatial factors linked to phosphorus loading in Manitoba’s portion of the Lake Winnipeg watershed.
I built a geospatial layer set and grouped it into:

Sources (wastewater treatment sites, agricultural land, phosphorus contamination risk)
Transport pathways (stream networks, waterbodies, drainage features like ditches/culverts)
Landscape factors (soil texture/drainage, clay content, erosion risk, wetlands, slope)

The final deliverable is an ArcGIS StoryMap and web map that lets you explore how these risk factors overlap across the region.
View the StoryMap

title: MGEM
text:
Severity
Example workflow used in the capstone project to classify burn severity from a dNBR raster.

RPython


# raster processing package
library(terra)

# load dNBR raster
dnbr <- rast("dnbr.tif")

# reclassification matrix (from, to, class)
rcl <- matrix(c(
  -Inf, 0.1, 1,   # unburned / low
  0.1, 0.27, 2,   # low-moderate
  0.27, 0.44, 3,  # moderate-high
  0.44, Inf, 4),  # high
  ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)

# classify dNBR into severity categories
severity <- classify(dnbr, rcl)


# numerical array operations
import numpy as np

# create output array for severity classes
severity = np.zeros_like(dnbr)

# reclassify dNBR values into severity categories
severity[(dnbr <= 0.1)] = 1
severity[(dnbr > 0.1) & (dnbr <= 0.27)] = 2
severity[(dnbr > 0.27) & (dnbr <= 0.44)] = 3
severity[(dnbr > 0.44)] = 4

title: Publications
Publications
text:
External links
You can also provide a frame linking to external websites. For example, here is a Google Earth Engine application I developed - which I embedded in this webpage using the code below:


<iframe width="900" height="700"
src="https://ee-melserramon.projects.earthengine.app/view/thermal-analysis-tool">
</iframe>

The full-screen GEE application is available here in case you’re interested.
(To use the GEE tool, navigate to any city you’d like, hit apply filters, and click anywhere on the map to retrieve a time-series of Landsat surface temperature observations for that point. Areas where the maximum temp exceeded 35 degrees Celsius in your date-range are highlighted in red.)

title: MGEM
section: Early GIS Project – Global Beer Price Map
text:
Early GIS Project – Global Beer Price Map
University of Winnipeg
This was the final map from the first GIS course I ever took. The course that introduced me to GIS in the first place. For the final project we could choose any topic as long as it showed what we learned throughout the term. I mapped average beer prices around the world because I want to travel more and because I like the occassional beer. After this course, I knew GIS was something I wanted to keep doing and it’s been a big interest of mine ever since!
VIEW MAP
